The morning came in grey and thin, the light pressing through cloud cover like something seeping. Maren stood at the window of her shop, watching the street empty itself of early shoppers. The rain had stopped but the cobblestones stayed dark with it. In the gutter, a paper bag had softened into pulp, the words STORGE BAKERY barely visible before they dissolved.

She hadn't slept. The back room still smelled of the tea she'd let go cold, sitting untouched on the workbench while she listened to the house settle around her. Old sounds. The bones of the building remembering older weights.

Her hands found the cold cup without her looking. She set it down again.

The ledger sat on the shelf behind the counter, spine turned inward, unmarked. She'd moved it three times in the night, telling herself she was checking for loose floorboards, loose seams in the walls. Telling herself she was being careful instead of being afraid.

The bell above the door chimed. Maren's hand went to the knife at her hip before she saw who it was, and then she made herself turn the motion into something else—adjusting the collar of her coat, reaching for a price tag she didn't need.

"You're closed," the woman in the doorway said.

"The sign says open."

"It's ten in the morning. You're never closed at ten."

Maren looked at her. The woman was younger than Maren by a decade, maybe more—hard to tell with the kind of faces that accumulated damage early and kept it. She wore a coat too heavy for the weather, the wool pilled at the elbows. Her hands stayed in her pockets. Her eyes kept moving to the corners of the room.

"I'm Wen," the woman said. "You know me."

"I know your name."

"You know more than that." Wen stepped inside, let the door fall shut behind her. "The safe house on Archer Street. The one that burned in '09. You were the one who warned my people before it happened."

Maren said nothing.

"I'm not here to collect. I'm here because Kellan found something. In the Hollow."

The name landed where it was meant to land. Maren felt it settle in her chest, the old cold recognition.

"Kellan should know better than to go digging."

"Kellan's dead."

The words came out flat. Wen pulled her hands from her pockets and Maren saw they were shaking—not much, a fine tremor, the kind that came from exhaustion or adrenaline crash.

"When?"

"Last night. Maybe this morning. They found him in the old Brennan plant. Upper floor." Wen's jaw tightened. "His throat was cut. Whoever did it left him where he'd be found."

"Why come to me?"

"Because he was looking for you. Because before he died, he sent a message to my people—a location. Coordinates for a vault in the sub-levels of the Hollow. Whatever he found, it's there."

Maren walked to the counter and picked up a cloth she used for dusting. She wiped the surface, which didn't need wiping. Her hands had stopped shaking years ago, but she remembered what it looked like from the outside.

"Why not go to the Arithmetician?"

"You know why."

She did. Dova Salter had no interest in the Hollow's secrets unless they could be tallied and owed. The condemned district existed in a kind of legal limbo—not outside Covenant law but beneath it, a place where old debts accumulated interest that no one bothered to calculate.

"The vault," Maren said. "Do you know what's in it?"

"No. Kellan was careful. He only shared the location after he was already in too deep to turn back." Wen's voice caught on something. "That's how he always was."

Maren set the cloth down. "And you want me to go look."

"I want you to come with me. I don't—" Wen's hands flexed at her sides. "I can't do this alone. Whatever's down there, I need someone who knows how to look without being seen."

The request hung between them. Maren heard the weight behind it—the woman asking for help was the same woman who had once watched three of Maren's targets walk into a building that caught fire an hour later. The same woman who had learned to be useful and stay useful and not ask questions about the cost.

"I'll need your car," Maren said.

***

The Hollow spread across the eastern edge of the industrial district like a wound that had stopped bleeding but never healed. Maren watched it through the passenger window as Wen drove, the buildings rising around them in silhouettes of rust and broken glass. The pigeons were visible on the rooftops, a grey mass of unmoving shapes. When they moved, they moved together.

"You've been here before," Wen said.

"Once. Years ago."

"What happened?"

Maren looked at a water tower that had rusted into a shape like a crown. "Something I don't remember wanting to remember."

Wen didn't ask follow-up questions. That was something, at least.

The Brennan plant occupied a block at the center of the district—five stories of brick and iron, windows like dead eyes, a loading dock that opened onto a courtyard choked with weeds. Police tape still circled the perimeter, yellow against grey. Someone had spray-painted a symbol on the wall near the main entrance: a circle bisected by a line. The Covenant mark for unverified debt.

"Here," Wen said. She pulled the car to the curb, killed the engine.

Maren looked at the symbol for a long moment. "That's not official."

"I know."

"Someone's trying to make a claim they can't back up."

"That's what Kellan was trying to figure out." Wen reached into her coat and pulled out a flashlight, a small one, the kind that fit in a palm. "He thought the mark was a fake. A distraction. He went looking for whoever made it."

Maren took the flashlight, tested the beam. Still bright. "And instead they made him into a message."

The courtyard smelled like rust and something else—something organic, sweet in a way that had gone past rot. Maren stepped over the tape without touching it and walked toward the loading dock. Behind her, she heard Wen following, heard the woman's breathing quicken as they crossed the threshold into shadow.

The interior of the plant was a cathedral of emptiness. Machinery still lined the walls, massive shapes draped in dust and cobwebs, their purposes forgotten. The floor was concrete, cracked in places where water had seeped up through the foundation. Light fell through the broken skylights above in thin columns, catching the motes that drifted through the stale air.

"Upper floor," Maren said. "Where they found him."

"I don't want to see—"

"You don't have to look."

They found the stairs at the back of the building, an iron staircase that groaned under their weight. Each step rang with a sound like a bell struck wrong. Maren led the way, her hand trailing along the railing, feeling for wobble, for give. The knife at her hip was familiar in her palm, the weight of it something she'd never quite gotten used to and had never quite let go of either.

The upper floor opened onto a corridor lined with offices, doors hanging open on rusted hinges. Most of the rooms were empty. A few contained furniture collapsed in on itself—desks, chairs, the bones of filing cabinets. One room had been set up as living quarters, at some point. A mattress on the floor, a hot plate, a stack of newspapers dated from six months ago.

Kellan had been in the last room on the left.

The body was gone, of course. But someone had left something behind. A circle drawn on the floor in chalk, the same symbol from the wall outside. Inside the circle, a pile of papers that had been arranged carefully, deliberately, like an offering.

Maren crouched at the edge of the chalk line and looked. Ledgers. Old ones, the binding cracked, the pages soft with age. She could see handwriting on the visible pages, numbers and names and dates that went back decades.

"These are founding documents," she said.

Wen crouched beside her. "The Covenant started here? In the Hollow?"

"Before it was the Hollow. This was the Brennan packing plant, but before that—" Maren picked up the topmost ledger, felt the weight of it. "This is the original ledger. The one that started everything."

"Someone's been looking for this for years."

"Someone found it." Maren set the ledger down, picked up the next. The handwriting changed across the pages—different hands, different eras, the same meticulous record-keeping. Names she recognized. Names she didn't. Debts transferred, collected, left unpaid. "Kellan was close to something. Close enough to die for."

"Why leave it here?"

Maren turned the ledger over in her hands. "Because he wanted it found. Because someone needed to know what was in the Hollow." She looked at the chalk symbol, the careful circle. "But this isn't Covenant work."

"What do you mean?"

"Anyone who knows how to read the marks would know this is wrong. The proportions are off. The line's in the wrong place." She stood, dusting off her knees. "This isn't a claim. It's a forgery."

"Someone's trying to—what? Start something they can't finish?"

Maren walked to the window, looked out at the courtyard below. The pigeons had begun to move, a slow spiral rising from the rooftops. She watched them until they dissolved into the grey sky.

"Someone's trying to start something they think they can control."

Behind her, Wen was gathering the ledgers, stacking them with hands that had stopped shaking. Practical hands. Hands that knew how to take hold of things and not let go.

"Can you read all of it?"

"Most of it. Some of it will take time."

"Then take the time." Wen straightened, the stack of ledgers held against her chest like something precious. "Kellan died trying to find this. The least we can do is finish what he started."

Maren didn't answer. She was looking at the chalk symbol again, the wrong proportions, the misaligned line. The work of someone who had learned the marks from secondhand sources, from whispers and guesses. The work of someone who wanted power they didn't understand.

The stairs rang with their footsteps as they descended. The loading dock received them back into grey daylight. The courtyard, the car, the drive back through the Hollow's silent streets. Wen talked about next steps, about who could be trusted, about the Arithmetician's inevitable response when she learned what had been found. Maren let the words wash over her without catching them.

At the edge of the district, she asked Wen to stop the car.

"What is it?"

"Take the ledgers to the address I'll give you. Don't open them. Don't show them to anyone."

"Where will you be?"

Maren opened the passenger door. "Looking for the forger."

She stood on the curb and watched the car pull away, the taillights fading into the grey. The pigeons had returned to their rooftops, motionless again. The Hollow held its breath around her.

She walked.

The streets here had names once—posted on rusted signs at the corners, barely legible beneath layers of paint and weathering. She read them as she passed: Forge Street, Calloway Lane, Abattoir Passage. The names of an industry that had moved on, leaving only the labels behind.

The building she was looking for stood at the end of Abattoir Passage, a narrow vertical house with external stairs of black iron stacked like books on a shelf. Five stories. Windows like dark eyes. A door at street level that had been painted red once and had faded to the color of dried blood.

She climbed.

The stairs groaned beneath her. The iron was cold through her shoes. At the third floor, a window had been broken and replaced with a piece of plywood, the edges sealed with tape that had yellowed with age. At the fourth floor, she stopped.

The door was open.

Maren drew the knife and pushed it open wider with the back of her hand. The room beyond was empty except for a chair, a table, a single lamp that wasn't lit. The walls were bare except for one thing: a mark drawn in chalk on the plaster, the same symbol from the Brennan plant, the same wrong proportions.

Someone had been here recently. The air still held the ghost of smoke, something cheap and acrid. On the table, a cup of tea gone cold, a film forming on the surface.

She stepped inside. Her foot found something on the floor—a piece of paper, folded once. She picked it up without touching the edges.

Inside, in handwriting she didn't recognize: a name, an address, a date from three days ago.

And below that, a single line: *She doesn't know what she started.*

Maren folded the paper and put it in her pocket. The tea on the table had gone cold. The lamp wasn't lit. The window showed her the Hollow spread out below, the pigeons on their rooftops, the grey sky pressing down like something that had learned patience.

Someone was playing a game they didn't understand. Someone had found the founding ledger and decided to use it as a template for something new. Someone had killed Kellan and left the ledgers behind like breadcrumbs, like a dare.

The Arithmetician would want to know. The Quartile would want to know. The whole fragile architecture of debt and transfer would shudder when it learned what had been found in the Hollow's secret places.

Maren walked back down the iron stairs. The cold pressed through her shoes. The pigeons didn't move.

At the bottom, she stopped. Looked up at the building she had just descended. The windows watched her, dark and patient.

She knew this building. She had been here before, years ago, when the iron was new and the paint was fresh and the business inside was something she had been sent to conclude. She remembered the stairs groaning under her weight. She remembered the door opening onto a room that smelled like ink and copper. She remembered a man who had asked her to wait while he finished writing something in a ledger, his hand trembling, his voice steady.

She didn't remember his name.

She didn't remember if she had been the one to—

Her hand went to the scar at her temple. The white line, precise and old. She pressed her fingers against it and felt nothing, the numbness that had settled there years ago, the place where sensation had simply stopped.

The paper in her pocket was a weight. The knife in her hand was a weight. The ledgers in Wen's car were a weight, traveling toward an address that would become another weight, another thread in a web she had spent fifteen years pretending she had escaped.

*I doesn't know what she started.*

Maren walked out of the Hollow. The streets released her into grey daylight. The cobblestones of the Tide Quarter welcomed her back with their familiar unevenness, the stones worn smooth by generations of feet that had all gone somewhere, all carried something.

She thought about the cold tea on the table. The smoke in the air. The chalk mark on the wall, drawn by hands that had learned from whispers and guesses.

She thought about Kellan's body, found where it would be seen. About the ledgers arranged like an offering inside a circle that meant nothing to anyone who could actually read it.

She thought about a name on a piece of paper, and a man she didn't remember, and the scar at her temple that she had never asked anyone to explain.

The shop was where she had left it. The bell above the door chimed when she entered. The ledger still sat on the shelf, spine turned inward.

She locked the door behind her. Turned the sign to CLOSED.

And stood in the grey light of morning, holding the piece of paper in her hand, reading the name she didn't recognize, wondering if she had finally found the thread that would unravel everything—or if the unraveling had started long before she thought, in a room that smelled like ink and copper, with a man whose name she had made herself forget.
